era_context: The Second Punic War (218–201 BCE) is in its final stages, with Rome on the verge of victory over Carthage. Scipio Africanus is preparing for the decisive campaign in North Africa. In the east, the Seleucid Empire under Antiochus III is expanding, and the Ptolemaic Empire is weakening. The Han Dynasty in China is consolidating power after the civil wars of the Chu-Han period. This is a year of military preparation and political transition.

events.title: Scipio Africanus invades North Africa (Second Punic War)
events.description: Scipio Africanus, the Roman general, invaded North Africa in 203 BCE, bringing the war directly to Carthage's homeland. This bold strategy forced Hannibal to abandon his campaign in Italy and return to Africa to defend Carthage. Scipio's invasion was a turning point in the Second Punic War, shifting the initiative decisively to Rome. The invasion demonstrated Roman military superiority and strategic innovation, and it set the stage for the final battle at Zama in 202 BCE. Scipio's campaign in Africa is considered one of the most successful military operations of the ancient world.
events.certainty_note: Multiple near-contemporary sources confirm Scipio's invasion of North Africa in 203 BCE. Livy and Polybius wrote within ~150 years. The date is secure within ±1 year.

events.title: Hannibal withdraws from Italy
events.description: In response to Scipio's invasion of North Africa, Hannibal withdrew his army from Italy in 203 BCE, ending his 15-year campaign on the Italian peninsula. Hannibal's withdrawal marked the end of the most dangerous phase of the Second Punic War for Rome. Although Hannibal had never been decisively defeated in Italy, he had been unable to break Rome's resolve or capture Rome itself. His withdrawal was a strategic necessity to defend Carthage against Scipio's invasion. The withdrawal is often seen as a tacit admission of Carthaginian defeat.
events.certainty_note: Multiple sources confirm Hannibal's withdrawal from Italy in 203 BCE. The date is secure within ±1 year.
cross_references: Follows from -203-001 (Scipio's invasion of North Africa)

events.title: Antiochus III continues expansion in the Levant
events.description: Antiochus III of the Seleucid Empire continued his military campaigns to recover territories that had broken away from Seleucid control. In 203 BCE, he was consolidating control over the Levant and preparing for the decisive campaign that would culminate in the Battle of Panium (200 BCE). Antiochus's ambitions to restore the Seleucid Empire to its former glory were becoming increasingly apparent, and his expansion was beginning to alarm both the Ptolemaic Empire and Rome.
events.certainty_note: Antiochus III's campaigns are well-attested, but the specific dating of individual campaigns is approximate. The year 203 BCE falls within his period of expansion, but no source explicitly dates a specific event to 203 BCE.

events.title: Han Dynasty: Kangxi's reign continues; consolidation
events.description: Emperor Kangxi of the Han Dynasty (r. 157–141 BCE) continued his reign during this period. The early Han Dynasty was consolidating power after the civil wars of the Chu-Han period. The year 203 BCE falls within the early consolidation phase of the Han Dynasty, a period of relative stability and prosperity.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, Chapters 10–12
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu, Chapters 1–4

events.certainty_note: Kangxi's reign is well-attested, but the year 203 BCE does not correspond to any specific recorded event. The certainty is 'probable' based on the broader context of his reign.
cross_references: Part of broader Han Dynasty consolidation period (-206 to -141 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: Some modern scholars debate the exact date of Scipio's invasion of North Africa, with some sources placing it in 204 BCE rather than 203 BCE. However, the majority of scholars (following Livy and Polybius) place it in 203 BCE. The significance of the invasion is not disputed. Additionally, some scholars argue that Hannibal's withdrawal from Italy was not as complete as traditionally portrayed; he may have left some forces behind to continue harassing Roman territories.
historiographic_note: The year 203 BCE is well-documented for the Mediterranean world through Greek and Roman sources (Livy, Polybius, Appian). The Second Punic War is one of the most extensively documented conflicts in ancient history. Chinese sources provide context for the Han Dynasty but are less detailed for this specific year. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic and social history are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Scipio's invasion of North Africa forced Hannibal to withdraw from Italy
Scipio's invasion of North Africa led to the Battle of Zama
Hannibal's withdrawal from Italy led to the Battle of Zama
